text: I'd very much like to know the details and outcome of these tests! What kind of timeline are you looking at?

Me too! 
I worked as a teacher in different coding bootcamps. I’m really interested to know more about it

> There is no question that better languages can drastically simplify programming..

I question it, actually.

1. I don't think programming can be simplified. There's a kernel of irreducible essential complexity here. What we _can_ do is unpack the complexity and stage it, so that newcomers don't have too steep a wall to climb. Carve steps into the sides of the wall.

2. I don't think the solution is better languages. The solution is better environments. We've recently discussed this here.

Totally fine that we disagree about these things! I just object to the "no question".

I toyed around with the idea of programming purely via voice, without any display, a few years ago. The goal would be to be able to build new voice based apps with just voice/chat. Gets quite impractical for even the simple cases, so perhaps a display is needed :slightly_smiling_face:.
Maybe something similar to Google Glass + voice based programming would already be usable. Then you could program while walking in the forest, for example :slightly_smiling_face:.

Yeah, Macromedia were wonderful. I still have my boxes of Macromedia Flash / Freehand,  (not to mention my boxes for Bryce 3D, Starcraft, and Photoshop.. 5? 4? Can't remember.)

The Adobe acquisition really stung, because Adobe software at the time felt comically unreliable and slow (especially compared to the pro 3D tools I was using). I hated the thought of Flash (which I loved from the get go) sharing that fate. And, lo and behold, that's exactly what happened. Adobe software is still unreliable, slow, and even more expensive than the $700 boxed versions (which is a lot of money to shell out all at once when you're a teenager — I can appreciate why the subscription model is nice — but as an adult I'd rather pay $700 every 2 or 3 years than $60/month).

Hi Nathan, welcome. I'm curious if you could elaborate on how you feel the pedagogy led your students wrong and what you think is the right approach to learning to create things with code?

Yes I'm probably using the word 'emergent' loosely to talk about expected behavior but specified somewhat indirectly.

> one approach I've seen a few times is to allow a system to be coded (or otherwise specified) as if it were one self contained program, which then either compiles into separate parts, or and acts like a CI/CD/build script which installs or updates separate components on each server.

Dan, I'm very interested in these, if you have specific pointers I'd really appreciate it. The thing I'm really looking for is not specifying 'as a program' but a system that has the notion of update built in as well. (One program that's running distributed gets complicated when you think of updating your program.)

Yes, Unison is interesting. Last I looked the focus was more on uniquely identifiable functions that can be run anywhere and not on specifying the higher level processes decoupled from the implementation topology. It's probably worth taking another look.

> Both groups are excluded at present, and the seniors have the most to offer because they know what should be built, while kids typically only know games

I think the tool that can make games well vs. the tool that can make "what should be built" is probably very different?  My guess is most serious applications that a senior might design look more like spreadsheets, or an ECA system, and wouldn't necessarily have the primitives to make compelling games or interactive art\stories that more kid focused environments like scratch or etoys encourage.  This comes back to <@UCUSW7WVD>'s comment that simplifying programming language doesn't help much, you need to build environments that empower people

One thing I've anecdotally experienced with seniors (relatively small sample size of my parents and their friends), is even if they use consumer applications daily, and have been using computers in some form for most of their lives, its common that they don't understand that they don't understand the limitations of modern computing devices, which is a big ask, but I wonder if a lot of great ideas seniors would come up with are probably the way things "should be" in computing, but are also 10x unsolved research problems stacked on top of each other :slightly_smiling_face:

this is one of the advantages of kids focusing on games, kids can be easily impressed with just moving a sprite on the screen or displaying some text, anything that allows for a little self-expression, imo seniors (and non-coding adults in general) have a higher bar

seniors are a little different in that they probably don't care about immediate employment like most adults that go to coding bootcamps do, so they would care more about what they can do to make their lives or their community better, vs younger adults that aren't retired that tend to think about "how much, javascript, python and sql can I learn to get a job"

A local nonprofit that works closely with the school districts in my state recruited me and others as college mentors to lead a new coding camp, with one college mentor per class of about 30 kids. They gave us a curriculum provided by a local boot camp. The result was that the entire 9-week program was dedicated to having each kid build the same website, with a bit of flexibility for styling. I had to fight the system to give the kids in my classroom freedom to pursue the projects they actually wanted to work on.

The perception among educators seems to be that because programming is considered a STEM field, it can be effectively taught like a math class. The teacher presents the material, the kids are assigned homework problems, and the teacher grades the resulting work. This is not how anyone that I know who loves programming learned to code. In my experience, coding is much better learned the way an artist learns to paint or a writer to write. Rather than giving the kids homework assignments specially crafted to be easy to grade, we should be giving them freedom to pursue whatever projects interest them, and provide guidance and support in helping them to choose projects that are achievable and will stretch them. I find that the things I learn actually stick with me if I discover them for myself.

Hi! I’m Ian (but you could tell that already), from Minneapolis.

I’m particularly interested in programming environments – for instance, notebook interfaces seem like the most notable programming exploration of the moment. But the specific project I’ve been mulling over and experimenting with is using fully automated testing (like fuzzing) of interactive applications to collect execution information to support the programmer. I imagine a system where you can get a map of what the application actually does (intentional or not), and then you can explore down into _why_ it does those things.

But it’s a side project, so maybe more dream than reality, but I’m sure I’m not alone :wink:

text: Nay — different audience, different goals. But I do support the idea of "user liberation". I intend that anyone should be _able_ to use the stuff I'm building, but most people wouldn't want or need to, since it solves a very particular problem having to do with communication media.

The word “activist” seems to require buying into a specific solution that you then go out to evangelize in the world.  I’m still in the research phase along 3 fronts:
• Trying to come up with a solution.
• Tracking the solutions others come up with from similar goals.
• Figuring out the right ways to evangelize a solution once I have it.
